text: A three-dimensional network solid composed of Fe(III) centers and paramagnetic semiquinoid linkers, (NBu4)2Fe(III)2(dhbq)3 (dhbq(2-/3-) = 2,5-dioxidobenzoquinone/1,2-dioxido-4,5-semiquinone), is shown to exhibit a conductivity of 0.16 ± 0.01 S/cm at 298 K, representing one of the highest values reported to date for a metal-organic framework (MOF). Through a combination of spectroscopic techniques, slow-scan cyclic voltammetry, and variable-temperature magnetic and conductivity measurements, we identify the origin of this high electronic conductivity as ligand mixed-valency. Notably, UV-vis-NIR diffuse reflectance measurements provide the first observation of Robin-Day Class II/III mixed valency within a MOF. To explore the relationship between ligand redox states and material properties, we synthesized the reduced framework Na0.9(NBu4)1.8Fe(III)2(dhbq)3. A comparison between the two compounds reveals significant differences in electronic conductivity and magnetic ordering temperatures, which we correlate to the relative ratio of the ligand oxidation states. These findings establish transition metal-semiquinoid systems as a powerful platform for achieving and tuning long-range electronic communication in metal-organic frameworks.
